10.1016/j.jmp.2025.102926
The present manuscript is the second part of a three-part work that suggests a way to unify three main theories of assessment originated in the different traditions of Psychometrics and Mathematical Psychology. The focus is on the connection of parametric, non-parametric, and multidimensional Item Response Theory (IRT, NIRT, and MIRT, see e.g.,  Van der Linden, 2016a, 2016b, 2018 ), Cognitive Diagnostic Assessment 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         Although CDA usually encompasses also MIRT models, the acronym is preferred to Cognitive Diagnostic Models (CDMs) or Diagnostic Classification Models (DCMs) as it allows to properly spell out sentences like “KST, IRT, and CDA models”. Sometimes, however, we will use the acronym CDM if needed. 
                       (CDA, see e.g.,  Rupp et al., 2010; von Davier & Lee, 2019 ), and Knowledge Structure Theory (KST, see e.g.,  Doignon & Falmagne, 1999; Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ). The reader is referred to Part I for an introduction to the works that have previously explored and bridged such theories. As in the first contribution, models are directly addressed by their acronyms, a list of which is given in  Appendix B .

The framework suggested in Part I integrates the traditional Generalized Latent Variable Models (GLVMs, see, e.g.,  Bartholomew et al., 2011; Skrondal & Rabe-Hesketh, 2004 ) approach with the set-theoretic approach of KST by providing a small set of two primitives (structure and process) and two operations (factorization and reparametrization) from which models can be derived. The concept of ‘structure’ is borrowed from KST, and captures the relation between atomic elements of a given ‘sort’ (e.g., items or attributes). The concept of ‘process’, which establishes a probabilistic relation between structures, is a generalization to structures of the conditional-probability-based traditional approach of GLVMs. The operation of ‘factorization’ is associated to some independence assumption like, for example, Local Independence (LI), and allows to rewrite a conditional probability as the product of multiple terms (the definition of which is dependent on the chosen form of independence). Finally, the operation of ‘reparametrization’ is associated to the notions of kernel and of link function that in GLVMs provide alternative functional forms to the conditional probabilities. Processes can be combined in different ways (e.g., sequentially or in parallel) thus providing ‘blueprints’ for families of models which are further specified via different choices of structures, kernels, and link functions. In Part I, this approach allowed to derive a taxonomy of models (see  Table 3 ) under which KST and early CDA approaches appear to be based on the same theory applied to atomic elements of different sorts and with factorized conditional probabilities, while modern CDA approaches appear to be discrete versions of IRT models, with conditional probabilities that are reparametrized versions of the conditional probabilities featured in KST and early CDA models. Aside from specific models like those discussed in  Heller et al. (2015) , and theory-specific taxonomies for KST (see, e.g.,  Falmagne & Doignon, 2011 ), CDA (see, e.g.,  Rupp & Templin, 2008; Rupp et al., 2010; von Davier et al., 2008 ), or IRT (see, e.g.,  Sijtsma & Hemker, 2000; Thissen & Steinberg, 1986 ), there is up to our knowledge no comprehensive account of the relations between the models of these theories.

However, bringing together different theories, with their associated traditions, notations, and nomenclatures inevitably leads to some degree of incongruence as well as to some clash of notations. This holds especially true for KST and CDA that, as pointed out by  Heller et al. (2015) , have sometimes inconsistent nomenclatures. Two simple examples of these inconsistencies are given by the notions of knowledge and competence states and by the use of the letters  
                         q 
                       and  
                         Q 
                      . Indeed, while the KST notions of knowledge and competence states respectively characterize a subset of items and a subset of attributes, the CDA notion of knowledge state characterizes an ‘attribute profile’ (i.e., a subset of attributes), while the KST notion of knowledge state is known as an ‘ideal response pattern’. Similarly, KST reserves the use of the letters  
                         q 
                       and  
                         Q 
                       for respectively an item  
                         q 
                       and its domain  
                         Q 
                      , while CDA reserves them for an element  
                         q 
                       of the  
                         Q 
                      -matrix and the  
                         Q 
                      -matrix itself. In what follows, we have tried to reduce these inconsistencies as much as possible by using a notation and nomenclature that, in spite of leaning mostly toward KST, tries to embrace the CDA nomenclature wherever possible. The choice of theoretically ground the framework on KST was deemed natural for at least two reasons: first, since KST is based on set-theory, it mathematically provides a more foundational perspective than CDA; second, KST has a single consistent tradition, while CDA has been “developed based on at least two, if not three traditions” ( von Davier & Lee, 2019 ). We understand that some CDA readers might rightfully disagree with the notation used or find it more difficult to identify the models they are familiar with, but we hope that they will understand our rationale and that any inconsistency, omission, or simplification is not meant to undermine the relevance of their approaches. More details on the notation for CDA models are provided in Section  4 .

At present, as a preliminary nomenclature, it is important to stress that the terms ‘skill’, ‘attribute’, and ‘ability’ are respectively common in KST, CDA, and IRT contexts. While the term skill is consistently used in KST, both the terms skill and attribute are common in CDA (see, e.g.,  von Davier & Lee, 2019 ). In the proposed framework we use skills and attributes to refer to dichotomous skills and attributes in the KST and CDA sense, while we favor the term ‘trait’ over ability when referring to continuous latent variables (see Part III). As a consequence, in this contribution, we will mostly use the terms skills and attributes interchangeably. Besides, we will not delve into the distinction between ‘mastering’ or ‘possessing’ a skill/attribute and the two terms will be also used interchangeably. Similarly, we will not use the term ‘master’ only in conjunction with items, as it is common in KST, since the ‘mastery of a skill/attribute of interest’ is a rather common notion in CDA (see, e.g.,  von Davier & Lee, 2019 ). Therefore the term master will be used for both notions of items and skills/attributes. We believe that no confusion should arise. A distinction between  mastering  and  solving  an item will be however introduced in the same spirit as in KST, to distinguish between the latent knowledge and the observable responses to the items (e.g., an individual might guess and solve an item that in principle they might not be able to master).

Given the previous notational remarks, to obtain the taxonomy in  Table 3 , a specific configuration of processes was suggested to provide the blueprint and the vocabulary to systematize most models. Specifically, a two-processes approach based on a sequential combination of a  
                         p 
                      -process and a  
                         g 
                      -process ( Hutchinson, 1991 ) was suggested. As summarized in the diagram of  Fig. 1 , the  
                         p 
                      -process moves from a  competence  layer (i.e., a structure based on a domain of skills/attributes) to a  knowledge  layer (i.e., a structure based on a domain of items/tasks). It is a common tenet of all KST, CDA, and IRT models that skills and attributes (the competence layer) provide the means to master specific items or groups of items (the knowledge layer). As the  
                         p 
                      -process assumes a skill-based approach to mastering items, an  informed guess  occurs if items are mastered in absence (or partial presence) of the adequate attributes. Similarly,  slipping  due to lack of information or confusion in presence of adequate attributes can occur, thus resulting in individuals failing to master items that they should master. The  
                         g 
                      -process moves instead from the knowledge layer to the actual  response  layer and is based upon pure chance so that individuals might solve or not items that they are supposed to master or not. The reader once again is referred to Part I for a more detailed introduction to the topic.

The framework introduced in Part I is here used to derive KST and CDA models when dichotomous latent variables are considered. Models belonging to these theories that require polytomous or continuous latent variables are postponed to Part III, although some of them are briefly discussed within a latent class perspective. In spite of the fact that CDA and KST were developed in distinct fields and with slightly different aims, they share remarkable similarities. CDA mostly aimed at assessing the competence level of individuals and bridging the gap between IRT modeling and cognitive theories (see, e.g.,  Mislevy, 1996 ), while the initial focus of KST was on predicting the pure performance of individuals over a series of questions in order to deliver an efficient computerized assessment. Yet with time both approaches framed their models in terms of discrete skills/attributes, thus focusing on discrete classification of individuals. Their main differences are more of a formal nature, with KST focused on set-theory, orders, and combinatorics, while CDA focuses on probabilistic models framed within a linear algebra notation. Equivalence of some of their models was shown by  Heller et al. (2015) , but it was already clear that several CDA models had no KST counterpart while the hierarchical approach provided by KST was rarely applied in CDA. Derivation of both theories within a unified framework thus achieves a two-fold goal:

(a) 
                            it clearly settles the relation between the two frameworks by showing that KST and early CDA models are different applications of the same primitives, while modern CDA strives toward ‘general models’ and ‘general frameworks’ that are reparametrized versions of the early models viewed through the lenses of link functions, 
                         
                         
                            (b) 
                            it provides a simultaneous generalization of both frameworks, thus providing the foundations for the analysis of more general models and situations. For instance, it extends KST models to probabilistic relations between attributes and items (a feature which is typical of CDA models), while introducing in CDA the KST concept of structure, which generalizes the traditional CDA approach of using the power set of the attributes.

it clearly settles the relation between the two frameworks by showing that KST and early CDA models are different applications of the same primitives, while modern CDA strives toward ‘general models’ and ‘general frameworks’ that are reparametrized versions of the early models viewed through the lenses of link functions,

it provides a simultaneous generalization of both frameworks, thus providing the foundations for the analysis of more general models and situations. For instance, it extends KST models to probabilistic relations between attributes and items (a feature which is typical of CDA models), while introducing in CDA the KST concept of structure, which generalizes the traditional CDA approach of using the power set of the attributes.

Exchange of notions and concepts between the theories becomes thus possible and sheds light on features and issues of both theories. As an example, the duality between some conjunctive and disjunctive models recently recognized and investigated in CDA (see, e.g.,  Chen et al., 2015; Köhn & Chiu, 2016; Liu et al., 2011; von Davier & Lee, 2019 ) is grounded in a well-known duality between knowledge structures obtained from disjunctive or conjunctive interpretations of the skill maps in KST (see Section  4.2 ). As another example, a unified approach might also aid the study of the identifiability issue of the models of these theories. For a recent review of the issue in KST see, for example,  Heller et al. (2024) , for a recent review of the issue in CDA see, for example,  Xu (2019) . Although the study of the identifiability issue is beyond the scopes of the present work, still it is reasonable to point out that within a unified framework one should be able to recover already known results while having the opportunity of discover more general ones. A promising result in such a direction, although not specific to KST and CDA but rather to KST and IRT, can be found in  Noventa et al. (2024)  that used the present framework to recover and expand the traditional IRT results on the identifiability of the 3-4 parameter models.

As to the plan of the present manuscript, after a summary of the concepts introduced in Part I (see Section  2 ), KST models are derived in Section  3 , while CDA models are derived in Section  4 . A general approach is discussed that encompasses CDA frameworks like GDINA and LCDM, as well as approaches like ECDM, which correspond to specific choices of the kernel associated to different link-functions within the reparametrization operation. The GDM and MGDM frameworks are postponed to Part III as they require polytomous and continuous variables. The kernel is specifically chosen to be a linear sum of coefficients associated to the states  
                         C 
                       of a competence structure. It is shown that such a kernel offers a simple way of classifying models based on the conjunctive or disjunctive nature of the problem function, which associates competence and knowledge states while generalizing the concept of  
                         Q 
                      -matrix. It is worth noticing that the same kernel used to derive CDA models is also used to derive IRT models in Part III. As a general organizer, models can be identified within the taxonomy later provided in  Table 3  by considering the following five general questions, which might also be considered as guidelines for practitioners to select a specific model for their problem at hand.

Q1 
                            Which atomic elements and structures are involved? 
                         
                         
                            Q2 
                            How many processes are involved? 
                         
                         
                            Q3 
                            Which assumptions are imposed on the processes? 
                         
                         
                            Q4 
                            Is there some independence assumption on the processes? 
                         
                         
                            Q5 
                            Which link function and kernel are chosen?

Which atomic elements and structures are involved?

How many processes are involved?

Which assumptions are imposed on the processes?

Is there some independence assumption on the processes?

Which link function and kernel are chosen?

The answer to Q1 establishes the atomic entities considered in a model and the associated structures. In the case of KST and CDA models, these are dichotomous items and attributes, and consequently knowledge and competence structures. Response structures based on items are considered for the response patterns. The answer to Q2 establishes the relations between the different structures. As it will be shown, two sequentially combined processes are sufficient to derive most KST and CDA models. The answers to Q3 and Q4 establish instead which assumptions are imposed on these processes, which is one of the main features characterizing different models. A fundamental assumption is whether processes are probabilistic (thus generalizing the traditional concept of latent class model) or deterministic (here represented by the KST notion of problem function recast as a generalized  
                         Q 
                      -matrix). Although Q4 is a sub-case of Q3, its relevance deserves a question on its own. Indeed, independence is one of the most important assumptions in KST and CDA models and is associated to the operation of factorization which allows to show that KST and early CDA models are based on the same theory applied to different atomic entities. Finally, the answer to Q5 characterizes the operation of reparametrization which is fundamental in modern CDA approaches and general frameworks. The choice of link function characterizes indeed different general frameworks, while the choice of which terms are retained in the kernel characterizes different families of models (specifically, disjunctive, conjunctive, and main effect models).

The present section summarizes notions introduced in Part I, to which readers are referred for more details. Readers already familiar with Part I can directly move to Sections  3 and 4 .

Eq.  (4)  combines Eqs.  (2) and (3)  in such a way that only the probability spaces underlying the processes

Eq.  (4)  acts like a blueprint by means of which specific models can be derived once specific structures and functional forms for the  
                            g 
                         - and  
                            p 
                         -process have been chosen. A taxonomy of models, here reproduced in  Table 3 , is obtained by identifying the most common choices in literature. All models that are not discussed in this article have been de-emphasized. The  
                            g 
                         -process, which corresponds in the psychometric literature to the presence of left-side added parameters ( Thissen & Steinberg, 1986 ), has three main cases (a) absence of a  
                            g 
                         -process, (b) knowledge-based  
                            g 
                         -process  
                            
                               
                                  P 
                               
                               
                                  X 
                                  | 
                                  K

                                  C 
                                  , 
                                  K

                            p

                            p 
                         -process (

                                     K 
                                     | 
                                     C 
                                  
                               
                               =

                                     K

                            p 
                         -process, (c)  
                            p 
                         -process in which no independence assumptions on the attributes are imposed, (d)  
                            p 
                         -process in which effective competence states are involved, and (e)  
                            p 
                         -process in which independence assumptions on the attributes are imposed. Characterization of these cases require the concepts of problem function/generalized  
                            Q 
                         -matrix, effective competence state, and Generalized Local Independence (GLI) which are now introduced.

A third concept, which is associated to the factorization operation, is LI. As it can be seen in  Table 3 , different models can be classified according to whether or not some form of independence is imposed on attributes and/or items. However, several definitions of LI have been given in literature and there is sometimes confusion between the different notions of ‘conditional independence’, ‘uni-dimensionality’, and ‘non-invasiveness’ of the item relations (see, e.g.,  Henning, 1989 ). In addition, alongside traditional forms of LI like conditional independence (strong form), or conditional covariance (weak form), one can also find generalizations like essential independence ( Stout, 1990 ), or local non-negative dependence ( Holland, 1981 ) among others. For a discussion of LI in the context of KST and IRT models, the reader is referred to  Noventa et al. (2019) . For a discussion on the inadequacy of LI as a definition of latent variables, the reader is referred to  Bollen (2002) . In what follows, we refer to the strong notion of LI as a statement of probabilistic independence of the observed responses

                               ∈ 
                               X

                               ∈ 
                               Y

GLI thus generalizes LI to learning spaces, which are necessary and sufficient to detail the relation between KST, CDA, and IRT models as in the Taxonomy in  Table 3 , since the most common structures used in CDA and IRT models (see Part III) are respectively the power set and the chain. This section connects KST to the setting of the M2PM. For a more detailed introduction into KST see  Doignon and Falmagne (1999)  or  Falmagne and Doignon (2011) . First, the original performance-based approach to KST is introduced, in which there is no focus on attributes/skills but only on the mastering or solving of items, then competence-based KST is discussed as both a deterministic and a probabilistic extension of performance-based KST.

Performance-based KST was extended by developing techniques to account for the skills underlying the items ( Doignon, 1994; Düntsch & Gediga, 1995; Gediga & Düntsch, 2002; Korossy, 1997, 1999 ). Given non-empty finite domains  
                            Q 
                          of items and  
                            S 
                          of skills, a  skill multimap  is a triple  
                            
                               ( 
                               Q 
                               , 
                               S 
                               , 
                               μ 
                               ) 
                            
                          where  
                            
                               μ 
                               : 
                               Q 
                               → 
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                        
                                           S 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          is a mapping that associates to every item  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                            
                          a non-empty collection  
                            
                               μ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          of non-empty sets  
                            
                               C 
                               ⊆ 
                               S 
                            
                         , containing the skills which are sufficient to master the item. The sets  
                            C 
                          are called  competencies . A skill multimap can assign more than one competency to an item as there might be multiple solution strategies. If it assigns only one competency to each item it is called a  skill map . It is instead called a  skill function  when the competencies are pairwise incomparable w.r.t. set inclusion. It is therefore rather intuitive to notice that skill functions and skill multimaps can be thought of and recast as collections of skill maps. Besides, skill functions are in a bijective correspondence with the problem functions  
                            
                               ( 
                               Q

As these models have been shown to be equivalent to polytomous and dichotomous IRT models, the most general versions of KST models encompass IRT models (see  Noventa et al., 2019  and Part III for further details).

The CDA literature comprises both CDMs and MIRT models (see, e.g.,  von Davier & Lee, 2019; Wang & Chang, 2016 ). Whereas MIRT models focus on latent traits (see Part III), many CDMs are concerned with discrete attributes. In what follows we aim at recovering the CDMs listed in  Table 3 . As mentioned in the introduction, since the notation used in the framework is KST-based, CDA readers might have some difficulties in recognizing the models they are familiar with due to some notational differences. In what follows, we have tried to reduce these differences as much as possible by using a notation and nomenclature that, in spite of leaning mostly toward KST, tries to embrace the CDA nomenclature wherever possible. In order to do so, we have considered the slightly different notations used in the authoritative books on CDA by  Rupp et al. (2010)  and  von Davier and Lee (2019)  and we opted to follow the notation used in the former since it has more symbols in common with the KST notation. Indeed, the probability of a response pattern

or in vector notation

                      . In essence, the probability  
                         
                            π 
                            
                               (

                                        q

                                  }

                       has been given the shape of Eq.  (6)  by assuming an effective competence state. In order to show how Eq.  (31)  encompasses most CDMs we first discuss the notions of kernel and  
                         Q 
                      -matrix in Sections  4.1 and 4.2 , then we use Eq.  (31)  to write a general form for CDA models in Section  4.3 . General frameworks like GDINA ( de la Torre, 2011 ) and LCDM ( Henson et al., 2008; Rupp et al., 2010 ) are shown in Section  4.4  to correspond to different reparametrizations, as argued by  de la Torre (2011) . CDMs equivalent to KST models are derived in Section  4.5 , namely the DINA ( Haertel, 1989; Junker & Sijtsma, 2001; Macready & Dayton, 1977 ) and DINO ( Templin, 2006; Templin & Henson, 2006 ) models. Their reparametrized versions RDINA ( DeCarlo, 2011 ) and RDINO ( Köhn & Chiu, 2016 ) are discussed. Considerations are also provided for the MS-DINA ( de la Torre & Douglas, 2008 ) and the HO-DINA ( de la Torre & Douglas, 2004 ), for the PINC and HO-PINC models ( Zhan et al., 2018 ), and for general approaches like ECDM ( Park et al., 2018 ). Models based on conjunctive and disjunctive problem functions are then derived. Conjunctive problem functions imply positive coefficients  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               k 
                               , 
                               T 
                            
                         
                       in the kernel  (32) , while disjunctive problem functions allow for negative coefficients  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               k 
                               , 
                               T 
                            
                         
                      . Models like the NIDA ( Junker & Sijtsma, 2001; Maris, 1999 ), the (Reduced) NC-RUM (or Red-RUM) ( DiBello et al., 1995; Hartz, 2002; Hartz et al., 2002 ), and the MCLCM-C ( Maris, 1999 ) are based on conjunctive problem functions, while disjunctive problem functions yield a class of models including the MCLCM-D ( Maris, 1999 ). Models that are often classified as compensatory, namely the NIDO ( Templin, 2006 ), the C-RUM ( Hartz, 2002; Templin, 2006 ), the R-RUM ( Hartz, 2002 ), the LLM ( Maris, 1999 ), and the ACDM ( de la Torre, 2011 ) are classified as  main effects  models since they can be derived from both conjunctive and disjunctive problem functions by setting all interaction coefficients in the kernel to null. For the derivation of general frameworks like GDM ( von Davier, 2005 ) and MGDM ( von Davier, 2008 ), and models like the Full NC-RUM and the Unified model, that require polytomous responses and attributes or continuous traits, the reader is referred to Part III.

Traditionally, a  
                            Q 
                         -matrix (see, e.g.,  Tatsuoka, 1990 ) is a binary matrix  
                            Q 
                          of elements  
                            
                               
                                  ξ 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                                  a 
                               
                            
                          that attain value one if an item  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               Q 
                            
                          requires an attribute  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     s 
                                  
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               S 
                            
                          to be mastered, that is  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     s 
                                  
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                            
                         , and zero otherwise.  
                            Q 
                          is given a  conjunctive  interpretation if for every item there is exactly one subset of skills  
                            
                               
                                  S 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          that are all needed to master it, or a  disjunctive  interpretation if each skill in  
                            
                               
                                  S 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          allows to master the item. The relation between the  
                            Q 
                         -matrices and the problem functions  
                            
                               ( 
                               Q 
                               , 
                               S 
                               , 
                               p 
                               ) 
                            
                          introduced in Section  2.3  is many-to-one, with every problem function corresponding to a collection of  
                            Q 
                         -matrices (see, e.g.,  Heller et al., 2015 ). This holds true because the  
                            Q 
                         -matrix is the CDA notion corresponding to the KST notion of skill map briefly introduced in Section  3.2 , while the problem functions are in a bijective correspondence with the skill functions, which can be thought of as collections of skill maps 
                            5 
                         
                         
                            5 
                            For the traditional definition of skill function as a triple  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     Q 
                                     , 
                                     S 
                                     , 
                                     f 
                                     )

                                     f

                                     Q

                                                 S 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                the interested reader is referred to, for instance,  Heller et al. (2013) . For the purposes of the present manuscript its characterization as a collection of skill maps, as pointed out by  Heller et al. (2015) , is sufficient.. 
                         . As mentioned in Section  2.3  a problem function

                                  C 
                                  )

                               K

                            Q 
                         -matrix, a problem function can be  conjunctive  ( disjunctive ) if all the associated skill maps are conjunctive (disjunctive).

As a final note, it is worth mentioning that conjunctive and disjunctive interpretations of standard  
                            Q 
                         -matrices are dual to each other in the following sense. It is a well-established Competence-based KST result that for fixed finite nonempty sets  
                            Q 
                          and  
                            S

                            Q

In the remainder of this section, we show that system (40) encompasses most CDA models and frameworks, which require all or some of the sub-equations in system (40). As it can be noticed, both reparametrizations recast the set of multiplicative attribute parameters  
                            
                               ( 
                               σ 
                               , 
                               γ 
                               ) 
                            
                          into new sets of additive parameters. The main difference in the reparametrizations is in the general function characterizing the conditional probability itself, in the logit case a softmax representation is chosen, in the log case an exponential representation is chosen. Before applying these reparametrizations to derive specific models, let us show that different choices of link function  
                            
                               
                                  ℓ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        r 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          set the general frameworks like GDINA or LCDM.

                          where a conjunctive (disjunctive) problem function yields the RDINA (RDINO). Recent CDA literature has focused on a duality between DINA and DINO (see, e.g.,  Chen et al., 2015; Köhn & Chiu, 2016; Liu et al., 2011; von Davier & Lee, 2019 ) consisting in the fact that reverse-coding of both data and attributes of a DINA model can be fit by a DINO model, and vice-versa. A typical proof is given by rewriting the RDINA as an RDINO for reverse-coded attributes and data. As shown in Section  4.2 , such a duality is rooted in the duality between knowledge structures delineated by conjunctive and disjunctive skill maps.

Finally, by means of the reparametrization operation a representation of DINA and DINO can be given within the GDINA and LCDM frameworks. In the context of the GDINA framework, setting

More in general, the coefficients given in  (79)  are a reformulation of those provided by  Chiu and Köhn (2016) .

                            . Contrary to the fact that the DINO is the disjunctive version of the DINA, the models resulting from Eq. (80)  is the MCLCM-D, where all items share the same attributes ( Maris, 1999 ). Although the DINO cannot be subsumed under the present model because assuming a single attribute would be meaningless, the pattern confirms the disjunctive nature of these models.                              on the logit scale are given by Eq.  (84) .

Compensatory models like NIDO, C-RUM, R-RUM, LLM, and ACDM are here considered  main effects models  as they are obtained by setting the interaction coefficients to null in the kernel of Eq. (40a) and can have both conjunctive or disjunctive problem functions. A notable example is given by the R-RUM that is a log–log reparametrization of the Reduced NC-RUM, which is based on a conjunctive problem function. The NIDO ( Templin, 2006 ) and the C-RUM ( Hartz, 2002 ) are commonly introduced in the LCDM notation ( Rupp et al., 2010 ). The R-RUM ( Hartz, 2002 ) is the C-RUM but with a log link function. The LLM ( Maris, 1999 ) is equivalent to the C-RUM ( Hartz, 2002 ). The ACDM was suggested by  de la Torre (2011)  in the context of the GDINA. The main differences between NIDO and C-RUM are a) the addition of a constraint for the NIDO that rewrites the intercept of the model into  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        λ 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∑ 
                                     
                                     
                                        a 
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           | 
                                           S 
                                           | 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        λ 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                        , 
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ξ 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            , and b) that the NIDO depends on attributes only, whereas the C-RUM depends on both attributes and items. The ACDM is the C-RUM/LLM with the identity link replacing the logit link while the R-RUM is the C-RUM with the log link replacing the logit link. Similarities and selection of these models were also discussed in  Ma et al. (2016) .                              where the logit-logit case follows from Eq.  (79)  while the log–log is the form attained by the R-RUM when the main effects are constrained using the log representation of the conjunctive Reduced NC-RUM. A similar reasoning can be followed for the disjunctive case. Finally, in order to retrieve the NIDO one needs to impose the constraint

                                        ξ 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                        a 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             on the intercept. In the identity link case this constraint means that the probability of a correct response given the empty competence state is a sum of probabilities associated to the single attributes. In the logit link case it means that the value on the logit scale associated to the empty set is the sum of the logit values associated to single attributes (hence the NIDO can be regarded as a discrete version of the LLTM in IRT discussed in Part III). In both cases, the NIDO constraint on the intercept is independent of the requirement of an additive model, and so does not affect the conjunctive or disjunctive nature of the model.

As a consequence of the framework introduced in Part I and summarized in Section  2  of the present manuscript, KST and CDA models for dichotomous items and attributes can be identified within the taxonomy provided in  Table 3  by considering the five general questions Q1-Q5 that have been listed in the introduction. General practitioners might then select (or construct) a given model by asking themselves which atomic elements and structures are involved (Q1), how many processes are involved (Q2), which assumptions are imposed on these processes (Q3 and Q4), and finally which link function and kernel are chosen (Q5). The latter question being especially relevant in CDA since it characterizes the choice of general framework and the conjunctive, disjunctive, or main effect nature of the chosen model.

Although CDA and KST models were developed in distinct fields and with slightly different aims, these theories share remarkable similarities in that they both classify individuals by means of discrete elements like items and attributes/skills. The differences between these theories are more of a formal nature, with KST focussing on set-theory, orders, and combinatorics, and CDA focussing on probabilistic computational models framed within a linear algebra notation. A systematic comparison between these theories was provided by  Heller et al. (2015) , in which correspondences between theories in terms not only of basic concepts but also of equivalent models were traced. The general framework introduced in Part I, which by nature merges and bridges set-theoretical and vector notations, was used here to derive KST and CDA models based on dichotomous latent variables, thus clearly establishing and settling the relation between the two frameworks, while simultaneously providing a generalization of both of them. As to KST models, it was shown that the BLIM corresponds to a pure  
                         g 
                      -process, while the CBLIM corresponds to adding to the BLIM a deterministic  
                         p 
                      -process provided by the generalized  
                         Q 
                      -matrix  
                         
                            
                               Q 
                            
                            
                               C 
                               , 
                               K 
                            
                         
                      . In addition, it was shown that the SLM corresponds to a generalized probabilistic independence assumption on the state probabilities. Finally, while the CBLIM corresponds to a deterministic competence-based extension of the BLIM, it was shown that a probabilistic competence-based extension of both SLM and BLIM allows to derive latent class versions of the  
                         Θ 
                      -BLIM and  
                         Θ 
                      -SLM suggested by  Noventa et al. (2019) , which are equivalent to (and generalize) IRT models and encompass other KST models like the LKS ( Stefanutti, 2006 ). For a detailed discussion of this part the reader is referred to Part III. After deriving the KST models, the focus was moved to showing how the concepts of local independence and link function can be used to derive the main CDA models and to switch from one framework to the other. In particular, it was shown that KST and early CDA approaches appear to be based on the same theory applied to atomic elements of different sorts and with factorized conditional probabilities, while modern CDA approaches appear to be discrete versions of IRT models, with conditional probabilities that are reparametrized versions of the conditional probabilities of KST and early CDA models. Indeed, by looking at  Fig. 3  it should now be clear that classic KST models essentially correspond to the  
                         g 
                      -process, while competence-based KST models essentially ignore the probabilistic nature of the  
                         p 
                      -process, CDMs instead are mostly focused on the  
                         p 
                      -process, with the DINA and DINO being the only exception, as they are the only CDMs that are equivalent to the KST CBLIM. More in details, KST models and early CDA models are just different applications of the same primitives of structure and process in conjunction with the factorization operation. While KST focused on a probabilistic process between responses and items (BLIM) at most supported by a deterministic process between items and attributes (CBLIM), early CDA models mostly focused on a probabilistic process between responses and attributes (thus bypassing the item level with the exception of DINA, MS-DINA, and DINO, in which said level is called ideal response pattern). In both theories, the factorization operation is applied to define guessing and slipping/careless error parameters. Modern CDA models strive instead toward ‘general models’ and ‘general frameworks’ (like the GDINA and the LCDM) that are in essence reparametrized versions of the early models viewed through the lenses of link functions and kernels borrowed from generalized linear models. It was indeed shown that the kernel given in Eq.  (22)  offers a simple way of classifying the CDMs based on the conjunctive or disjunctive nature of the KST problem function: in presence of local independence, indeed, models which are based on conjunctive problem functions (e.g., DINA, NIDA, NC-RUM, and MCLCM-C) are such that all their kernel coefficients  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                               , 
                               C 
                            
                         
                       are greater or equal than zero while models based on disjunctive problem functions (e.g., DINO, MCLCM-D) admit negative values for  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                               , 
                               C 
                            
                         
                      . A third class of main effects models, in which the interactions  
                         
                            
                               λ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                               , 
                               C 
                            
                         
                       are set to zero, contained those models that can be built from both conjunctive and disjunctive problem functions (e.g., NIDO, C-RUM, LLM, R-RUM, and A-CDM). Most importantly, all models were derived within a ‘general model’ provided by the system of Eqs. (40), which encompasses also models like PINC, HO-PINC, and HO-DINA, as well as general frameworks like GDINA and LCDM, and approaches like ECDM. The GDM and MGDM frameworks are postponed to Part III since they require polytomous items and polytomous/continuous attributes.

Some final remarks are in order. First, unification of the frameworks allows to generalize both KST and CDA. As previously mentioned, KST models were extended to probabilistic instead of deterministic relations between attributes and items, a feature which is typical of CDA models, while the KST concept of structure allows in line of principle for generalization of the computational CDA approach that traditionally considers only the power set of attributes in place of arbitrary structures. A further generalization of CDA concepts, in light of a KST perspective, was also provided by the definition of the generalized  
                         Q 
                      -matrix, which extends the notion of  
                         Q 
                      -matrix to the relation between knowledge states and competence states instead than between single items and attributes. Second, exchange of notions and concepts between the theories allows to shed light on features and issues of both theories. As an example, in Section  4.2  the duality recently recognized and investigated between some conjunctive and disjunctive models in CDA (see, e.g.,  Chen et al., 2015; Köhn & Chiu, 2016; Liu et al., 2011; von Davier & Lee, 2019 ) can be clearly framed in a well-known duality between knowledge structures obtained from disjunctive or conjunctive interpretations of the skill maps in KST. As another example, a unified approach might also aid the study of the identifiability of the models of these theories. Although as mentioned in the introduction studying this issue is beyond the scope of the present work, let us briefly mention that the present framework contributes to expanding the traditional IRT results on the identifiability of the 3-4 parameter models (see, e.g.,  Noventa et al., 2024  to which the reader is referred for a more detailed discussion). Third, the same kernel here used to derive CDA models will be used in Part III to derive IRT models by introducing latent traits as realizations of random variables over continuous structures, a result that highlights that CDMs are essentially discrete versions of IRT models (see also  DiBello et al., 2007; Formann, 1985; Heinen, 1993; von Davier, 2005 ). Finally, for KST and CDA models that are connected to polytomous or continuous latent variables the reader is also referred to Part III.

                          so that for competence states that contain more than one attribute the sum over the attributes disappears.
